note: Category 10. Investigated 2026-07-03 by a dedicated read-only agent AFTER the main workflow (safety+build investigators had failed on the session limit). Self-verified (agent re-read each cited line) but NOT independently adversarially verified.
summary: The safety architecture is unusually mature for a hobby project: the failsafe FSM, arm gate, ESC boot-arm, and per-field link2 staleness projection are all pure, well-tested state machines, and main.cpp forces throttle=neutral/steering=center/DRS=closed BEFORE and INDEPENDENT of every feature path each control tick, with ERS boost proven multiplicative so it cannot bypass the arm gate. The team has already found and documented most of the classic pitfalls (boot-full-lock A1, arm-into-throttle A2/A3, LEDC duty-0 idle A4, static-init arm-timer A5, RP1 'Set Position' failsafe A8) in docs/ROADMAP.md, and the D8 bench runbook gates ESC power behind proven failsafe. The scariest RESIDUAL risks are almost all hardware/config truths that no code can enforce: the ESC signal pin (GPIO14) is floating/high-Z in the tens-of-ms between reset and ledcAttachPin with no external pull-down specified, the 2000 ms ESC arm-hold and the forward/brake ESC mode are unverified guesses against the actual QuicRun 10BL120, and a control-board brownout/reset re-runs the ESC boot-arm but relies entirely on the RX 'No Pulses' failsafe to keep the RX from holding old positions. Battery monitoring is correctly warn-only with no auto-cut path. Board #2 has a proper independent dead-man and 500ms staleness->hazard, with the one caveat that a never-connected board #1 leaves it in a calm 'breathe' animation forever rather than escalating (cosmetic, engine stays silent).
well_designed: Failsafe is applied first and unconditionally each control tick: main.cpp:311 computes state before any actuation, and the Safe branch (main.cpp:340-348) forces steering=0, esc.setThrottle(0), drs.setOpen(false) with no early-return, so link2 keeps transmitting the failsafe flag but no feature code can write outputs in that branch.

FailsafeStateMachine.cpp:15 gates linkValid on everReceivedFrame_ (latched true on first frame, never reset per header:62) so timestamps alone can never fake a healthy link at boot -- this is the fix for the documented A1 boot full-lock bug, and drop-to-Safe is immediate with no debounce (the safety-critical direction).

Arm gate correctly enforces 'neutral observed since arming': ArmGate.cpp:8-9 clears seenNeutralSinceEnable_ on !armSwitchOn OR forceDisarm, and main.cpp:318-319 passes forceDisarm=(state==Safe) every tick, so every failsafe episode forces a fresh throttle-neutral before the motor can run again (closes A3).

ERS boost cannot bypass the arm gate: ErsSystem::applyBoost (ErsSystem.cpp:56-66) is purely multiplicative and returns shapedThrottle unchanged when input <=0, so applyBoost(0)==0; baseCommanded is already gated (main.cpp:328 = (active && armed) ? modeShaped : 0) before boost is applied at main.cpp:356.

ESC boot-arm hold is anchored to the first setThrottle() call (EscOutput.cpp:13-16), not to static-init construction, correctly fixing documented finding A5; and it is non-blocking (clock-based via injected IClock, no delay()).

Esp32LedcPwm::begin() writes an explicit safe pulse immediately after attach (Esp32LedcPwm.cpp:12) so outputs never idle at LEDC duty 0, and setup() (main.cpp:180-184) hands each channel its known-safe position (center/neutral/closed) independent of call ordering (fixes A4).

Battery monitoring is strictly warn-only: BatteryMonitor sets a latching warning_ flag (BatteryMonitor.cpp:58) with hysteresis + time-qualification, and no code path anywhere reads lowVoltageWarning() to cut throttle or steering -- it only feeds the link2 lowBattery field and CRSF telemetry.

Board #2 has an independent dead-man AND link staleness failsafe: audioTask forces synth silence if the control loop heartbeat is >500ms stale (main.cpp:71-72), and Link2Monitor zeroes commands/rpm and asserts failsafe after 500ms of no CRC-valid frame (Link2Monitor.cpp:31-54), with a never-connected boot default of failsafe=true (Link2Monitor.cpp:8).

Control loop is fully non-blocking: the only while() in loop() is the bounded UART drain (main.cpp:215), tick guards use phase-accumulating last=now (main.cpp:304) so a stall cannot burst-catch-up, and the documented worst-case failsafe latency is ~540ms vs a 500ms budget.

The D8 bench runbook (docs/D8_BENCH_BRINGUP.md) gates ESC power behind Phase 5 failsafe+arm proof, mandates RP1 'No Pulses' failsafe (A8), servo-center-before-linkage, and ESC forward/brake mode -- the operational safety envelope is written down.

findings.title: ESC signal pin (GPIO14) is floating/high-Z from reset until ledcAttachPin runs; no external pull-down specified
findings.locator: src/main.cpp setup() lines 173-181 (order: crsfUart.begin/link2Uart.begin/batteryAdc.begin/hallSensor.begin THEN escPwm.begin); Esp32LedcPwm.cpp:9-13; docs/w17_wiring_assembly_atlas.html ELEC-04 (no signal pull-down)
findings.evidence: escPwm.begin(escConfig.neutralMicros) at main.cpp:181 is the first moment GPIO14 is configured as an LEDC output; it runs AFTER four other begin() calls including hallSensor.begin() (attachInterrupt) and batteryAdc.begin(). Before ledcAttachPin, GPIO14 is a default input (high-Z). The wiring atlas power/signal diagrams (ELEC-02/ELEC-04) show one signal wire each to ESC/servos with no mention of a signal-line pull-down resistor.
findings.why_matters: From power-on until setup() reaches escPwm.begin (tens of ms after the bootloader + static init), the ESC signal line floats. A powered, already-armed ESC seeing a floating/noisy signal can interpret spurious pulses; GPIO14 is also an ESP32 default output-capable pin but is NOT a strapping pin, so the bootloader itself is benign -- the risk is purely the undriven window plus any ESC that latches on garbage. Same class of risk for steering GPIO13.
findings.expected: ESC signal held at a defined safe level (or the ESC not powered) throughout boot; ideally an external pull-down/RC on the ESC signal line so a floating pin reads as sub-neutral / no-pulse.
findings.suspected: Firmware cannot drive the pin before ledcAttachPin; safe boot relies on (a) the ESC's own power-on-neutral-arming requirement and (b) the D8 rule to not power the ESC until failsafe is proven. On the finished car both boards power together, so the ESC IS powered during this window.
findings.how_to_verify: Scope GPIO14 from the instant of 3.3V rail-up through the end of setup() on real hardware; confirm the ESC does not twitch. Confirm whether the QuicRun requires a clean neutral before it will arm (most Hobbywing ESCs do, which mitigates this).
findings.fix_direction: Add a hardware pull-down (or small RC) on the ESC and steering signal lines so the floating boot window reads as a safe/no-pulse level; optionally move escPwm.begin to the very first line of setup(). Document in the atlas.

findings.title: ESC boot-arm hold of 2000 ms is an unverified guess against the actual Hobbywing QuicRun 10BL120
findings.evidence: EscOutput.hpp:14 comment: 'hold neutral this long ... before accepting any non-neutral command (CLAUDE.md section 6.3)'. The value 2000 has no cited source tying it to the QuicRun 10BL120 arming spec; D8_BENCH_BRINGUP.md Phase 7 says 'firmware holds neutral ~2 s before accepting throttle; ESC arms' -- an assumption, not a measured requirement.
findings.why_matters: If the ESC needs longer than 2s to arm (or needs the throttle range calibrated first), the first throttle command after 2s could be ignored or, worse, the ESC could still be in its calibration/beeping state and misread the first non-neutral pulse. Too short = unreliable arm; the value being a guess means the exact behavior is unknown until bench.
findings.expected: bootArmHoldMs matched to the QuicRun 10BL120's documented power-on arming time (and range-calibration handled in the ESC's own config, as D8 notes).
findings.suspected: 2000 ms is a reasonable typical value and likely fine, but unproven; the ESC's arming is really governed by its own firmware, and the firmware hold only guarantees the ESC sees neutral first.
findings.how_to_verify: Bench: power ESC + firmware, confirm the ESC completes its arm tones within 2s of neutral and that the first post-hold throttle command actually spins the motor (wheels off ground).
findings.fix_direction: Confirm from the QuicRun manual / bench and set bootArmHoldMs accordingly (tunable via console in the tuning build).

findings.title: Control-board brownout/reset silently re-runs boot-arm and depends entirely on RX 'No Pulses' to avoid stale-position hold; no explicit brownout config
findings.locator: platformio.ini [env:esp32dev] (no brownout/WDT flags); src/main.cpp setup() boot sequence; failsafe everReceivedFrame_ resets to false on reset
findings.evidence: No brownout-detector or watchdog build flags or sdkconfig overrides exist anywhere (grep found only ROADMAP/D8 mentions). On any reset, everReceivedFrame_ (FailsafeStateMachine.hpp:62) returns to false, so the board correctly boots into Safe and re-runs the 2s ESC boot-arm -- good. But the outputs during the reset transient depend on the ESC/servos holding their last commanded PWM (no pulses arriving) and on the ESP32's default brownout reset behavior, which is not configured here.
findings.why_matters: The two-UBEC design (clean rail for ESP32, isolated ESC BEC) means the ESP32 rail can brown out while motor power stays up (or vice-versa). If the ESP32 resets mid-drive, the ESC keeps its last throttle for the servo-pulse-timeout of the ESC (typically it goes to neutral after ~ loss of signal, but this is ESC-dependent), then the firmware re-arms over 2s. During that window the car may coast at the last commanded throttle. The RX 'No Pulses' setting (A8) does not help here because board #1, not the RX, drives the ESC.
findings.expected: Defined, safe output state through a control-board brownout/reset; ideally the ESC configured to cut to neutral on signal loss faster than the firmware reboot, and the ESP32 brownout detector left at its safe default.
findings.suspected: Firmware handles the reboot correctly (boots Safe, re-arms); the transient behavior is entirely hardware/ESC-dependent and unverified.
findings.how_to_verify: Bench: sag the ESP32 rail (or reset the board) while commanding throttle with wheels off the ground; measure how long the motor keeps spinning and whether the ESC returns to neutral on signal loss. Confirm the ESP32 default brownout reset triggers cleanly.
findings.fix_direction: Verify the ESC's signal-loss timeout behavior; ensure adequate bulk capacitance on the clean rail; consider explicitly documenting/asserting the brownout detector level.

findings.title: Steering servo endpoints default to 500-2500 us with no verified mechanical travel limit; firmware can command a bind/stall
findings.locator: ServoConfig minMicros=500, maxMicros=2500 (lines 10-11); ServoOutput::setPosition clamps to these endpoints (ServoOutput.cpp:24-28)
findings.evidence: ServoConfig defaults min=500/max=2500 us (a full 180deg for the DS3235SG). setPosition maps normalized +/-1000 linearly onto center+/-(max-center) and clamps only to the physical 500/2500 endpoints -- there is no per-installation travel limit. The DS3235SG is a 35 kg-cm servo; the steering linkage (turnbuckles/knuckles per atlas MECH) will bind well before 180deg of servo travel.
findings.why_matters: Commanding full stick can drive the steering servo hard against the linkage stops, stalling a high-torque servo (heat, current draw, stripped gears / snapped tie-rod -- the atlas notes turnbuckles 'snap in a crash'). This is a real mechanical-damage path on a gift car. The firmware provides trim but no travel-limit endpoints below the servo's physical max.
findings.expected: Steering min/max set to the mechanical travel that the linkage allows, not the servo's full 500-2500 us range.
findings.suspected: Defaults are the servo's full range; correct endpoints must be dialed in at the bench (D8 Phase 6 says 'full left/right doesn't bind the linkage or stall the servo against the stops' but the default config will bind until narrowed).
findings.how_to_verify: Bench Phase 6: with the linkage fitted, sweep full left/right and confirm no bind/stall; narrow minMicros/maxMicros (steering config is NOT console-tunable per main.cpp:158 comment -- only center/trim are, so this requires a code edit + reflash).
findings.fix_direction: Set conservative steering endpoints in ServoConfig for the actual linkage travel; consider exposing steering min/max to the tuning console since only center/trim are currently tunable.

findings.title: CRSF telemetry is written to the same UART2 that receives RC frames; a blocking or backed-up TX could delay the RC drain
findings.locator: Esp32CrsfUart uses Serial2 for both begin(RX+TX) and write(); telemetry writes at main.cpp:268/279/293 (5 Hz), RC drain at main.cpp:215
findings.evidence: Esp32CrsfUart::begin (Esp32CrsfUart.cpp:12) opens Serial2 with both rxPin and txPin; write() (line 24) does Serial2.write on the same port. main.cpp writes three telemetry frames (~10-15 bytes each: battery, GPS, flightmode) every kTelemetryPeriodMs=200ms. Arduino HardwareSerial.write blocks only if the TX ring buffer fills; three small frames at 420000 baud clear in well under 1 ms, so blocking is very unlikely.
findings.why_matters: Any blocking on the CRSF UART would stall loop() and delay the RC drain / failsafe tick. The write sizes are tiny relative to the 420k baud rate and the 200ms cadence, so in practice this is safe, but it is a shared-resource coupling worth noting -- and if telemetry frame sizes grow (e.g. adding fields) the TX buffer headroom should be re-checked.
findings.expected: RC reception never delayed by outbound telemetry.
findings.suspected: Effectively harmless at current frame sizes/rate; flagged as a latent coupling, not a live bug.
findings.how_to_verify: Bench: scope loop timing while telemetry is active; confirm no periodic jitter in the control tick aligned with the 200ms telemetry cadence.
findings.fix_direction: None required now; keep telemetry frames small and infrequent, and re-verify TX buffer headroom if fields are added.

findings.title: Board #2 stays in calm 'breathe' animation forever if board #1 is never connected (never escalates to hazard)
findings.locator: LightRenderer::render, neverConnected branch (lines 70-81) vs localFailsafe hazard branch (lines 84-90)
findings.evidence: When link==NeverConnected the renderer shows a calm teal breathe and returns (lines 70-81), explicitly commenting 'a genuine cut wire reads as Lost -> hazard below, not NeverConnected forever on a powered link.' Engine is silent in this state (EngineSim sees armed=false -> Ignition::Off -> engineRpm=0). Only a link that was once Up and then went stale escalates to amber hazard (lines 84-90).
findings.why_matters: If board #1 is dead/unpowered/UART-miswired from the start, board #2 shows a benign 'waiting' animation indefinitely rather than an alarm. This is a diagnostics/UX gap, not a motion-safety issue: with board #1 dead the car cannot move and the engine is silent. The design comment acknowledges the trade-off (a truly cut wire mid-run reads as Lost->hazard, which is the dangerous case).
findings.expected: Either escalate NeverConnected to a distinct alert after some timeout, or accept the calm breathe as intentional 'not yet linked' UX.
findings.suspected: Intentional design; benign because a never-connected board #1 implies a non-moving car with silent engine.
findings.how_to_verify: Bench Phase 9: power board #2 alone; confirm silent engine + breathe animation; then connect and cut board #1 mid-run to confirm the Lost->hazard path fires within 500ms.
findings.fix_direction: Optional: after N seconds of NeverConnected, escalate to a distinct slow amber to aid wiring diagnostics. Not required for safety.

findings.title: Hall wheel-speed ISR on GPIO35 (no Schmitt trigger, slow edge) relies on a fixed 2ms software lockout for EMI rejection
findings.locator: onEdge() kLockoutUs=2000 debounce (header line 36, cpp lines 23-24); attachInterruptArg RISING (cpp:11)
findings.evidence: The header (Esp32HallPulseCounter.hpp:16-21) explicitly notes GPIO35 has no Schmitt trigger and 'ESC EMI riding a slow edge can double-count', mitigated by a 2ms ISR lockout ('9x margin' vs the ~18ms minimum real pulse spacing at top speed). onEdge uses relaxed atomics for count/period.
findings.why_matters: Wheel speed feeds only telemetry, ERS harvest logic, and the link2 rpm field -- NOT the throttle/steering/failsafe path. So even if EMI corrupts the count, it cannot cause unsafe motion; worst case is a wrong speed readout or spurious ERS harvest. The 2ms lockout is a reasonable software mitigation but a slow noisy edge could still register false RISING interrupts within the 2ms if two bursts straddle the window. The D8 runbook already flags scoping the Hall line and adding 1-10nF.
findings.expected: Clean, debounced Hall edges; EMI does not corrupt the count.
findings.suspected: Software lockout is adequate for mild bounce; heavy motor EMI may still need the hardware RC filter D8 mentions. No safety impact regardless.
findings.how_to_verify: D8 Phase 8: scope the Hall line at full throttle near the motor; check for double-counts; add 1-10nF if the edge is ugly.
findings.fix_direction: Add the hardware RC filter on the Hall output if bench scoping shows EMI double-counts; software lockout already in place.

findings.title: DRS 'closed = safe' and forward/brake ESC-mode assumptions are correctness-by-configuration, not enforceable in firmware
findings.locator: DrsOutput.hpp:9-12 (closedMicros=1000 assumed 'closed'); gearbox/Gearbox.hpp:57-61 + shapeThrottle brake pass-through (Gearbox.cpp:14); Link2Sender.cpp:35 reverse=false
findings.evidence: DrsConfig assumes closedMicros=1000 is the mechanically-closed wing position ('Closed is the failsafe-safe position' DrsOutput.hpp comment) -- but if the servo/linkage is installed reversed, 1000us could be fully OPEN, so failsafe would deploy the wing. Separately, shapeThrottle passes x<=0 through unshaped (Gearbox.cpp:14) and the code assumes the ESC is in forward/brake mode (comment lines 57-61); Link2Sender hardcodes reverse=false (line 35). In forward/reverse mode, sub-neutral PWM would be reverse and ungoverned by the gearbox.
findings.why_matters: These are 'the code is only correct if the hardware is wired/configured to match' assumptions. A reversed DRS servo makes failsafe open the wing (cosmetic, low risk). A wrong ESC mode (forward/reverse instead of forward/brake) means the 'brake' stick region drives full ungoverned reverse -- a real drivability/safety surprise. The D8 runbook Phase 7 correctly mandates forward/brake mode, so this is covered operationally but not enforced.
findings.expected: DRS closed direction verified at install; ESC confirmed in forward/brake mode per D8.
findings.suspected: Correct as long as the D8 bench checklist is followed; firmware has no way to detect a reversed servo or wrong ESC mode.
findings.how_to_verify: Bench: confirm DRS 1000us = wing closed (flip openMicros/closedMicros or the linkage if not); confirm ESC set to forward/brake (D8 Phase 7).
findings.fix_direction: Verify at install per D8; consider making DRS open/closed swap a config so a reversed servo is a config edit not a re-wire.

open_questions: Does the Hobbywing QuicRun 10BL120 return its output to neutral (motor stop) on signal loss, and how fast? This determines the coast behavior during a control-board reset/brownout.
What is GPIO14 (ESC) and GPIO13 (steering) actual logic level during the reset->ledcAttachPin window on the real board, and does the powered QuicRun twitch on it? No external pull-down is specified.

Is the ESP32 hardware task watchdog left enabled at its Arduino default? If loop() ever wedged (it shouldn't -- no blocking calls found), only board #2's dead-man protects the sound; the control board itself has no explicit WDT-driven safe-output on a hang.
Confirm the RP1 disconnect-declaration latency at the chosen packet rate plus the LQ=0 burst timing (D8 Phase 2) actually keeps worst-case failsafe detection within the ~540ms the firmware budgets.
With both boards on the shared 'clean' BEC #1 rail, does board #2's WS2812 inrush (1000uF strip cap charging) or I2S amp draw sag the rail enough to brown out board #1 at power-on?

hw_validation_items.item: Scope GPIO14 (ESC) and GPIO13 (steering) from rail-up through end of setup(); confirm no ESC twitch in the floating pre-ledcAttachPin window; add signal pull-down if needed
Set RP1 failsafe mode to 'No Pulses' and characterize the LQ=0 burst + disconnect latency (D8 Phase 2) -- the whole frame-timeout failsafe depends on this
Full Phase-5 failsafe+arm proof with NO motor power: arm-off neutral, arm-into-throttle blocked, TX-off centers/neutralizes, recovery requires fresh neutral, LQ=0-while-frames-flow drops to safe
Confirm ESC configured forward/brake (NOT forward/reverse) and that the 2000ms boot-arm hold matches the QuicRun's arming time; verify first post-hold throttle spins the motor (wheels off ground)
Narrow steering minMicros/maxMicros to the linkage's mechanical travel; sweep full left/right with linkage fitted to confirm no bind/stall on the DS3235SG
Confirm DRS 1000us = wing mechanically closed (failsafe-safe); swap open/closed config or linkage if reversed
Brownout/reset test: sag the ESP32 clean rail (or reset) mid-throttle with wheels off ground; measure motor coast time and ESC signal-loss behavior
Scope the Hall line (GPIO35) at full throttle near the motor for EMI double-counts; add 1-10nF RC filter if the edge is ugly
Cut the link2 UART mid-run and confirm board #2 enters hazard + engine-off within 500ms; power board #2 alone and confirm silent engine + breathe (never-connected)
Battery ADC two-point calibration at ~6.5V and ~8.4V; confirm the warn-only threshold (7000mV) and that NO path cuts throttle/steering on low battery
Power-on both boards together and confirm no rail sag / brownout from servo stall spikes (1000uF servo cap) or WS2812 inrush on the shared clean rail
